overview.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Web server services such as emails and databases, Shadow IT, Poor implementation of access controls, Authentication issues
overview.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Web, Phishing, Vulnerability exploit, Account takeover, Brute-force attack, Distributed Denial of Service(DDoS)

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.kev: CVE-2012-0754 [CWE-22]: Microsoft Word Remote Code Execution Vulnerability
stage.description: The Pikabot Distribution February 2024 group targets employees in the transportation industry with spear-phishing emails containing malicious attachments exploiting the CVE-2012-0754 vulnerability in Microsoft Word.

stage.name: Execution
stage.kev: CVE-2025-21418 [CWE-94]: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')
stage.description: Upon successful execution of the phishing attachment, the threat actors exploit a vulnerability (CVE-2025-21418) to inject malicious code using a command and scripting interpreter, enabling remote code execution.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: The attackers create a scheduled task to maintain persistence on compromised systems, allowing them to execute commands and steal credentials at predefined intervals.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.surface: Web server services such as emails and databases
stage.description: Threat actors exploit weaknesses in public-facing applications to escalate privileges and gain unauthorized access to sensitive data and systems.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 636 - Hiding Malicious Data or Code within Files
stage.description: To evade detection, the attackers hide malicious files or data within seemingly innocuous files or directories, taking advantage of poor access control implementations.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: Finally, the threat actors launch a DDoS attack against communication services, disrupting operations and causing potential financial losses for the targeted transportation companies.

overview.summary: The Pikabot Distribution February 2024 campaign targets the transportation industry by initially gaining access via spear-phishing, then escalating privileges and maintaining persistence before ultimately disrupting communication services.
